missing_perspectives.who: Malaysia's estimated 2.2 million documented migrant workers and the broader foreign workforce who send remittances to Indonesia, Bangladesh, Nepal, the Philippines and other countries
missing_perspectives.what: Migrant workers are likely the largest Malaysian user base for off-platform USD stablecoins. They use USDT and USDC to bypass traditional remittance fees (which can reach 7% through formal channels) and settle in minutes rather than days. The analysis frames stablecoin adoption entirely through the lens of domestic investors on licensed exchanges, but the largest and most welfare-relevant adoption is almost certainly among foreign workers sending money home — a use case invisible to both the SC approved-list and BNM's ringgit-only pilots.
missing_perspectives.why: This perspective matters because it reveals that the stablecoin ban does not prevent the activity it ostensibly targets — it merely pushes the most economically vulnerable users into unregulated, unprotected channels. The policy choice to exclude USD stablecoins from licensed exchanges may increase, not decrease, the risks borne by migrant workers.

missing_perspectives.who: B40 households across all ethnic communities, particularly the approximately 490,000 households (6.2% of population) below the national poverty line with sharp rural-urban and regional disparities in Sabah
missing_perspectives.what: B40 households — especially in rural Sabah, Sarawak, Kedah, and Kelantan — are simultaneously the most vulnerable to currency substitution risk (they have the least buffer against ringgit depreciation) and the least likely to benefit from any stablecoin innovation (they have the lowest digital and financial literacy). The analysis frames the issue as a regulatory-architecture problem for the financial system as a whole, but the distributional consequences are not neutral: the wealthy can hold dollars through formal channels; the B40 cannot.
missing_perspectives.why: Without this perspective, the analysis risks being a technocratic regulatory critique that ignores who bears the costs of both the current policy and the proposed alternatives. Any solution that does not address B40 financial inclusion fails the Madani economic framework's own equity commitments.

missing_perspectives.who: Small and medium enterprises (SMEs), particularly those engaged in cross-border trade who hold receivables in foreign currency
missing_perspectives.what: Malaysian SMEs face an RM101 billion financing gap (as acknowledged by BNM in its tokenisation discussion paper). SMEs importing goods from China or exporting to the US face currency mismatch risk and high cross-border settlement costs. Stablecoins offer a potential bridge — faster settlement, lower forex spread — but the regulatory vacuum means SMEs must either forgo the efficiency or use unregulated channels. BNM's 2025-2028 tokenisation roadmap explicitly identifies SME supply chain financing as a use case, but none of the three 2026 pilots addresses cross-border USD settlement.
missing_perspectives.why: The SME perspective reveals a contradiction in BNM's own policy: the central bank identifies SME financing as a priority use case for tokenisation, yet the regulatory architecture blocks the very instrument (USD stablecoins) that would most directly serve cross-border SME needs.

missing_perspectives.who: Youth under 30 — particularly Gen Z and younger Millennials who represent the fastest-growing segment of cryptocurrency users in Malaysia and globally
missing_perspectives.what: A 2025 Bitget Wallet survey found 36% of Gen Z use crypto for daily purchases, gaming and travel. In Malaysia, Luno's user demographics skew young — the 25-39 age group is the most active cohort. Youth see stablecoins not primarily as speculative assets but as practical tools for cross-border payments, freelancing income in foreign currency, and participation in the global digital economy. The regulatory architecture the analysis critiques was designed by and for an older generation that views digital assets through a securities-regulation lens.
missing_perspectives.why: This perspective matters because it recasts what looks like a regulatory-technical problem as a generational one. The policy choices made today will affect whether Malaysian youth build their digital financial lives inside or outside the regulated perimeter.

missing_perspectives.who: Women — who constitute only 23% of global crypto owners, with only 4.5% of adult women owning crypto compared to 15% of men in some surveys
missing_perspectives.what: The analysis is entirely gender-blind. In Malaysia, Luno reported a 17% increase in female customers in 2024 (versus 14% for male). Women represent 40% of Malaysia's tech workforce but are severely underrepresented in Web3, blockchain, and AI leadership. The stablecoin regulatory vacuum may have gendered effects: women are more risk-averse investors, more likely to rely on regulated channels, and therefore more likely to be excluded from the dollar-stablecoin market entirely — not because they don't want access, but because the regulated perimeter excludes the product.
missing_perspectives.why: Without a gender lens, the analysis cannot identify whether the regulatory architecture has differential impacts on women's access to dollar-denominated digital assets, financial autonomy, and protection from currency depreciation.

missing_perspectives.who: East Malaysian communities — particularly in Sabah and Sarawak where poverty rates are 3-4x the national average and where the digital payments infrastructure is less developed than in the Klang Valley
missing_perspectives.what: The analysis treats 'Malaysia' as a uniform jurisdiction, but the digital divide between Peninsular Malaysia and East Malaysia is stark. As of 2023, internet penetration stood at 97.4% nationally but rural areas trailed at 90.3%. In Sabah, hardcore poverty persists. BNM's ringgit stablecoin pilots are all institutionally focused (Capital A, Maybank, CIMB) with no East Malaysian participation announced. The stablecoin question looks very different from Kota Kinabalu or Kuching than from KL Sentral.
missing_perspectives.why: A Kuala Lumpur-centric regulatory analysis misses that the people most in need of alternative financial rails — rural East Malaysians with limited bank branch access — are the least likely to be served by BNM's current approach of institution-led, ringgit-only pilots.

missing_perspectives.who: Stateless persons and refugees in Malaysia — an estimated 180,000+ refugees registered with UNHCR who are formally excluded from the banking system
missing_perspectives.what: Refugees in Malaysia cannot open bank accounts. In February 2025, TNG Digital and UNHCR partnered to allow refugees to access financial services via TNG eWallet using UNHCR cards. For this population, stablecoins accessed through self-custody wallets may represent one of the few available mechanisms to store value in a stable currency and make cross-border transfers. The regulatory vacuum that the analysis critiques is not a bug for this population — it is the only feature that allows them any access at all.
missing_perspectives.why: This perspective complicates the analysis's implicit preference for bringing stablecoins inside the regulatory perimeter: for the stateless, the unregulated perimeter is the only perimeter they can access.

missing_perspectives.who: The Chinese-educated SME community — particularly family businesses in Penang, Johor, and Selangor engaged in regional trade networks across ASEAN and Greater China
missing_perspectives.what: Many Chinese Malaysian SME owners maintain cross-border trade relationships that involve multi-currency receivables and payables. They face the same USD-stablecoin question from a different angle: if competitors in Singapore, Thailand, and Hong Kong can settle trade invoices in USDT or USDC through regulated channels, Malaysian SMEs operating through unregulated channels face a competitiveness disadvantage and counterparty risk.
missing_perspectives.why: This perspective highlights that the stablecoin question is not abstract — it affects real trading relationships and Malaysia's position in regional supply chains.

alternative_framings.current: The analysis frames the SC's exclusion of USD stablecoins from the approved list as a policy choice that removes BNM's visibility over dollar-stablecoin flows — a regulatory blind spot.
alternative_framings.alternative: The SC's exclusion of USD stablecoins may be better understood as a direct consequence of BNM's ringgit non-internationalisation policy (RNI), not a separate or independent policy choice. Since 1998, BNM has maintained that the ringgit is a non-internationalised currency — offshore ringgit trading (e.g., NDF markets) is prohibited. Allowing USD/MYR trading pairs on licensed exchanges would effectively create an onshore USD/MYR market outside BNM's direct foreign exchange administration framework. The SC's stablecoin exclusion is therefore not an oversight or a 'ban' — it is the Securities Commission respecting BNM's constitutional mandate over currency and foreign exchange.
alternative_framings.evidence: BNM Assistant Governor Adnan Zaylani stated on 18 November 2025: 'The ringgit being non-internationalised is one protection that we have and that would already prevent the kind of destabilising forex market that we had in 1998.' BNM's FEA rules prohibit offshore ringgit trading in any form. The SC's Digital Assets Guidelines operate within this constraint — permitting USD-stablecoin trading pairs on licensed DAX would create MYR→USDT→USD pathways that would functionally circumvent the RNI framework.

alternative_framings.current: The analysis frames the absence of BNM telemetry on MYR→USD-stablecoin off-ramp volume as a regulatory failure — 'Bank Negara cannot count it.'
alternative_framings.alternative: BNM may have chosen not to publish the figure, rather than being unable to count it. On-chain data is publicly available and analysable. BNM's Digital Asset Innovation Hub, launched June 2025, and the 30-35 stablecoin applications BNM has received suggest the central bank has substantial visibility into the ecosystem. The non-publication of off-ramp volume may be a deliberate choice — publishing the number would quantify the very regulatory gap the policy framework creates, potentially undermining BNM's stated position that existing tools remain effective.
alternative_framings.evidence: BNM Governor Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour stated at Forum Ekonomi Malaysia 2026 that BNM had received 'approximately 30 to 35 applications regarding stablecoin and digital asset innovations' and that 'stablecoins and digital assets are expected to remain part of the financial ecosystem.' BNM's own Annual Report 2020 acknowledged that 'new forms of digital assets such as Stablecoin have also started to emerge.'

alternative_framings.current: The analysis presents Singapore's 2023 stablecoin framework as a benchmark Malaysia has failed to match, with the implication that MAS's approach is more 'open' than BNM's.
alternative_framings.alternative: Singapore's framework covers single-currency stablecoins pegged to SGD or any G10 currency including USD. However, MAS has not yet fully operationalised the framework as of early 2026. More importantly, Singapore is a global financial centre that actively internationalises the SGD. Malaysia is a small open economy that has maintained a ringgit non-internationalisation policy since the 1998 Asian Financial Crisis. The two central banks face fundamentally different structural constraints. What works for MAS — permitting USD stablecoins within a regulated perimeter — may be inconsistent with BNM's statutory obligation to safeguard the ringgit's stability under a managed-float regime with restrictions on offshore ringgit trading.
alternative_framings.evidence: BNM's FEA policy explicitly states: 'Ringgit remains as a non-internationalised currency, thus any offshore trading of ringgit such as ringgit non-deliverable forward (NDF) is not recognized.' MAS's August 2023 media release states: 'The stablecoin regulatory framework will apply to single-currency stablecoins (SCS) pegged to the Singapore Dollar or any G10 currency.'

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Thailand's Securities and Exchange Commission added USDT and USDC to its list of approved cryptocurrencies for trading pairs on digital asset exchanges, effective 16 March 2025. Exchanges can now facilitate direct trades between digital tokens and any approved cryptocurrency, reducing the previous requirement to convert to THB first.
international_parallels.lesson: Thailand demonstrates that an ASEAN peer can approve USD stablecoins on regulated exchanges without triggering currency substitution or capital flight — provided the Bank of Thailand maintains its separate restrictive stance on using crypto for payments. The Thai model separates trading approval from payment authorisation, which Malaysia could adopt.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: RBI Governor Sanjay Malhotra stated on 20 November 2025 that India will maintain a guarded stance on cryptocurrencies and stablecoins, arguing that India's existing digital payment rails (UPI, NEFT) already address the gaps that advanced economies seek to fill with crypto regulation. RBI Deputy Governor T. Rabi Sankar stated on 13 December 2025 that stablecoins pose significant risks to monetary sovereignty and that India has 'no reason to include stablecoins into its financial systems.' India is leaning towards not creating legislation to regulate crypto, preferring partial oversight.
international_parallels.lesson: India's RBI makes an argument directly relevant to Malaysia: that a country with robust domestic real-time payment infrastructure (India has UPI; Malaysia has DuitNow) may not need to accommodate private stablecoins to achieve financial inclusion or payment efficiency. This is a coherent counter-argument to the analysis's implicit assumption that approving USD stablecoins is the inevitable direction of travel.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Vietnam's Resolution 05/2025/NQ-CP (September 2025) bans the issuance of fiat-backed stablecoins and tokenised securities nationally. Resolution 222/2025/QH15 grants Da Nang exemptions for pilot programmes. The high capital requirements resulted in zero applicants for the pilot as of October 2025.
international_parallels.lesson: Vietnam demonstrates the risk of over-restriction: a complete ban on fiat-backed stablecoins, combined with high entry barriers, has produced a pilot with zero applicants — creating the very unregulated, invisible market the policy sought to prevent.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: The South African Reserve Bank's November 2025 Financial Stability Review identified crypto assets and stablecoins as a new risk to financial stability. Stablecoin trading volumes soared to nearly R80 billion (US$4.6 billion) by October 2025, up from less than R4 billion in 2022. The SARB warned that the borderless nature of crypto assets allows them to circumvent South Africa's exchange control laws, which currently do not cover digital assets.
international_parallels.lesson: South Africa is the closest parallel to Malaysia's structural problem: an emerging market with exchange controls that stablecoins can circumvent, where the central bank has publicly identified the regulatory gap but has not yet closed it. The SARB's explicit warning about exchange-control circumvention validates the analysis's core concern.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas extended its freeze on new Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) licenses indefinitely as of 2025, while simultaneously allowing Coins.ph's Philippine Peso stablecoin (PHPC) to exit its regulatory sandbox. The Philippines processed US$38.341 billion in personal remittances in 2024, the fourth-largest remittance market globally.
international_parallels.lesson: The Philippines demonstrates a dual-track approach: tight licensing control over VASPs combined with an authorised peso-pegged stablecoin for remittances — directly addressing the migrant-worker use case that Malaysia's ringgit-only pilots have not yet targeted.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: As of mid-2025, Turkish residents can hold and trade stablecoins via licensed crypto-asset service providers regulated by the Capital Markets Board (SPK), but the use of crypto-assets for payments is banned. Turkey enacted its crypto-asset law on 2 July 2024, and from March 2025 the SPK began full supervision of crypto-asset service providers.
international_parallels.lesson: Turkey offers a model where stablecoin trading is permitted but payments are prohibited — similar to Thailand's approach — which could allow Malaysia to bring USDT/USDC within the regulated perimeter for investment purposes while maintaining the ringgit-only restriction for domestic payments.

counter_arguments.counter: The '22 approved tokens' framing is outdated. As of March 2026, Luno alone lists 51 SC-approved digital assets across eight categories including Layer 1, Layer 2, DeFi, AI, gaming, NFTs, real-world assets, and DePIN. The SC's approval list is dynamic, not static. The original analysis's use of '22 tokens' understates the SC's willingness to expand the approved list — though it remains true that no USD stablecoin has been added.

The Standard Chartered model projects US$1 trillion in EM deposit flight, but the IMF's December 2025 'Understanding Stablecoins' departmental paper notes that currency substitution risk is most acute in countries experiencing high inflation, weak institutions, or diminished confidence in local currency. Malaysia does not meet these criteria: inflation has been moderate, BNM's institutional credibility is intact, and the ringgit is backed by US$125.53 billion in reserves as of end-December 2025. The mechanism may apply in theory, but the conditions for acute digital dollarisation in Malaysia are not present.

The GENIUS Act's on-chain freeze capability is a consumer-protection and law-enforcement feature, not merely a sovereignty concern. The same freeze power that allows OFAC to block illicit wallets also protects Malaysian stablecoin holders from theft and fraud. A regulated USDT/USDC market under Malaysian oversight could incorporate equivalent freeze mechanisms under Malaysian legal process. The analysis frames OFAC's reach as a loss of sovereignty, but the alternative — no freeze capability at all — would leave Malaysian users more exposed to crime.

BNM's three MYR-only sandbox pilots may be a deliberate sequencing strategy, not a policy gap. The central bank has publicly stated it intends to provide 'greater clarity on the use of ringgit stablecoins and tokenised deposits by end-2026.' BNM has also received 30-35 stablecoin applications — far more than the three announced pilots. The sandbox approach may be BNM testing ringgit tokenisation first (where it has clear jurisdiction) before addressing foreign-currency stablecoins (where jurisdiction is shared with SC and international regulatory coordination is needed). Sequencing domestic before cross-border is a standard regulatory approach.

The reframe presents a binary: on Malaysia's regulated rails, no dollar stablecoins; on unregulated rails, Washington holds the freeze button. But this overlooks a third possibility: Malaysia could license USD stablecoin trading while retaining ringgit-denominated settlement, as Thailand has done. Under such a model, Malaysians could buy and sell USDT/USDC on regulated exchanges, but all fiat on/off-ramps would remain in ringgit through licensed banks, preserving BNM's visibility over the MYR-USD conversion point. The regulatory architecture is not limited to the binary the reframe constructs.

missing_facts.fact: As of March 2026, Luno Malaysia lists 51 SC-approved digital assets — not approximately 22. The SC approved 29 new assets for Luno in March 2026, expanding the list across eight categories including real-world assets (RWA) and DePIN. No USD stablecoin is among them.

Thailand's SEC added USDT and USDC to its list of approved cryptocurrencies for exchange trading pairs, effective 16 March 2025. Thailand is the first ASEAN jurisdiction to explicitly approve USD stablecoins for trading on regulated exchanges.

BNM has received approximately 30 to 35 applications regarding stablecoin and digital asset innovations, and Governor Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour has stated that stablecoins 'will be there to stay, that we need to embrace.'

Approximately 97% of the global US$300+ billion stablecoin market references the US dollar, with concentration in USDT and USDC, per the IMF's December 2025 'Understanding Stablecoins' departmental paper.

Malaysia's five digital banks reached 2.4 million customers and RM4.2 billion in deposits by end-2025, with about 65% of customers from unserved and underserved groups (low-income households, gig workers, youth), per BNM's Annual Report 2025.
An estimated 3.1% of Malaysia's population (over 1.0 million people) owned cryptocurrency as of 2025, compared to 14% in Singapore and 20.54% in Vietnam.

The IMF's December 2025 'Understanding Stablecoins' report found that USD-backed stablecoins enabled more than US$170 billion in cross-border payments in 2025, compared to approximately US$125 billion in Bitcoin and Ethereum combined, and that stablecoin flows between emerging market and developing economies now account for the largest share of the market by value.

South Africa's stablecoin trading volumes soared to nearly R80 billion (US$4.6 billion) by October 2025, up from less than R4 billion in 2022, with the SARB explicitly warning that crypto assets can circumvent exchange control laws.

Malaysia's FATF 2025 mutual evaluation resulted in all 40 FATF Recommendations being rated 'largely compliant' or 'compliant' for the first time, with the report noting progress in 'risk-based supervision for financial institutions and virtual assets services providers (VASPs).'

The Centre for Market Education, a Malaysian think tank, issued a statement in January 2025 recommending the adoption of Bitcoin, asset-backed stablecoins, and a rejection of taxes on alternative currencies, while warning against CBDC implementation that could undermine currency diversity and individual privacy.

Over 160,000 migrant workers in Malaysia receive their wages directly into TNG eWallet accounts, with cumulative salary disbursement of US$1.21 billion (RM4.8 billion) in 2025. TNG Digital and UNHCR partnered in February 2025 to allow refugees registered with UNHCR to access financial services via the eWallet.

gender_gaps.missing: The analysis does not consider whether women — who are more likely to be financially excluded, more risk-averse in investment choices, and less represented in crypto markets globally — are differentially affected by the exclusion of USD stablecoins from regulated exchanges. Women who might benefit from stablecoin access for remittances, savings, or protection against currency depreciation are pushed into unregulated channels or excluded entirely.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Luno Malaysia reported a 17% increase in female customers in 2024 (versus 14% for men), and women represent 40% of Malaysia's tech workforce. If women are more likely to use regulated channels than unregulated ones, the SC's exclusion of stablecoins from regulated exchanges has a gendered impact. Globally, women make up only 23% of crypto owners (2025 Nordic Crypto Adoption Survey). A gender-blind regulatory architecture risks widening this gap.

gender_gaps.missing: The analysis does not consider the specific experiences of female migrant domestic workers — one of the largest groups of foreign workers in Malaysia — who use remittance services and could benefit from lower-cost stablecoin remittance channels. These workers face compounded vulnerabilities: gender, migrant status, and often informal employment arrangements.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Domestic workers are among the most financially excluded populations in Malaysia. For this group, stablecoin-based remittances accessed through mobile phones could represent a significant improvement over cash-based informal channels, but only if regulatory clarity exists.

economic_gaps.missing: The analysis frames the stablecoin question through a regulatory-architecture lens (what BNM can and cannot see) but does not address the class dimension: who benefits from dollar exposure and who does not. Wealthy Malaysians can hold USD through formal banking channels, offshore accounts, and foreign property. The B40 cannot. USD stablecoins are, for many B40 Malaysians and migrant workers, the only accessible dollar-denominated store of value. The SC's exclusion of stablecoins from regulated exchanges therefore has a regressive distributional effect: it blocks the poor from accessing the same currency diversification the wealthy achieve through formal channels.
economic_gaps.why_matters: Without a class analysis, the regulatory critique is incomplete. The question is not merely whether BNM has visibility over stablecoin flows, but whether the current policy framework entrenches financial inequality by restricting dollar access to those who already have it through formal means.

economic_gaps.missing: The analysis does not address the ringgit's structural vulnerability as a non-internationalised, managed-float currency of a small open economy. BNM's foreign exchange administration rules — particularly the ringgit non-internationalisation policy — create structural incentives for Malaysians to seek dollar exposure outside the formal banking system. If residents cannot freely convert MYR to USD through formal channels beyond certain limits, and if offshore ringgit markets are prohibited, then USD stablecoins fill a gap that the policy framework itself creates.
economic_gaps.why_matters: This framing shifts the analysis from 'BNM has a visibility problem' to 'BNM's own policy framework generates demand for the very instrument it has excluded from regulated exchanges.' The stablecoin question is endogenous to Malaysia's foreign exchange administration, not an exogenous technological shock.

geographic_gaps.missing: The analysis contains no East Malaysia dimension. Sabah and Sarawak have different digital infrastructure, poverty profiles, and financial inclusion challenges than Peninsular Malaysia. As of 2023, internet penetration stood at 97.4% nationally but rural areas (concentrated in Sabah and Sarawak) trailed at 90.3%. The World Bank noted in 2025 that 490,000 households remain below the national poverty line 'with sharp rural-urban and regional disparities, especially in Sabah.'
geographic_gaps.why_matters: The analysis's regulatory-architecture framing assumes a uniform national financial system, but the reality is that East Malaysians interact with the financial system differently — fewer bank branches, more reliance on informal channels, and greater potential utility for mobile-based stablecoin solutions. BNM's MYR-only stablecoin pilots involve no East Malaysian institutions or use cases.

geographic_gaps.missing: The analysis does not address the rural-urban digital divide. Rural Malaysians — across all ethnic communities — have lower digital literacy, less reliable internet access, and lower confidence in digital financial applications. Data from the Department of Statistics Malaysia indicates that only 71.4% of rural women feel confident using digital financial applications without assistance, compared to 89.2% in urban areas.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Stablecoin policy is not neutral across geography. Rural populations are simultaneously more vulnerable to ringgit depreciation (lower incomes, less diversified assets) and less equipped to navigate unregulated crypto channels safely. The SC's exclusion of stablecoins from regulated exchanges, combined with the absence of financial education for rural populations, creates a protection gap that disproportionately affects rural communities.

verification_log.my_addition: Luno Malaysia lists 51 SC-approved digital assets as of March 2026
Thailand SEC approved USDT and USDC for trading pairs, effective 16 March 2025
BNM received 30-35 stablecoin applications and Governor said stablecoins 'will be there to stay'
97% of global stablecoin market references US dollar per IMF December 2025 paper
Malaysia's five digital banks reached 2.4 million customers with 65% from underserved groups
USD stablecoins enabled $170B+ in cross-border payments in 2025 vs $125B in BTC+ETH
South Africa stablecoin volumes soared to R80 billion by October 2025 from less than R4 billion in 2022
Malaysia achieved all 40 FATF Recommendations rated 'largely compliant' or 'compliant' in 2025
Centre for Market Education recommended adoption of Bitcoin, asset-backed stablecoins, and rejecting CBDC
160,000+ migrant workers receive wages via TNG eWallet with $1.21B in salary disbursement in 2025
BNM's ringgit non-internationalisation policy as structural constraint on USD stablecoin approval
The SC's stablecoin exclusion may reflect BNM's RNI policy rather than an independent SC choice
verification_log.primary_source: my own reasoning — the analysis does not connect the SC approved-list to BNM's FEA/RNI framework

verification_log.my_addition: The GENIUS Act freeze capability can be reframed as consumer protection, not merely sovereignty loss
verification_log.primary_source: my own reasoning — the analysis frames OFAC reach exclusively as sovereignty concern

verification_log.my_addition: Malaysia could license USD stablecoin trading with ringgit-denominated settlement, per Thailand model
verification_log.primary_source: my own reasoning — synthesis of Thailand SEC model and Malaysian regulatory architecture

verification_log.my_addition: BNM's MYR-only pilot sequencing may be deliberate rather than a policy gap
verification_log.primary_source: https://www.bernamabiz.com/news.php?id=2520611 (BNM Governor statement) combined with my own reasoning about sequencing

overall_assessment: The original analysis is a well-sourced, structurally coherent critique of the regulatory blind spot created by Malaysia's exclusion of USD stablecoins from licensed exchanges. It scores approximately 54/100 on completeness. Its principal strengths are: accurate characterisation of the SC approved-list exclusion, correct identification of the GENIUS Act's extraterritorial freeze reach, and a well-constructed reframe around visibility versus absence. Its principal gaps are: (1) it is entirely missing the perspectives of the actual users of off-platform stablecoins — migrant workers, B40 households, SMEs, East Malaysians, women, and stateless persons; (2) it does not connect the SC's stablecoin exclusion to BNM's ringgit non-internationalisation policy, which is arguably the binding structural constraint; (3) it contains no gender, class, or geographic analysis; (4) it cites the SC approved list as '22 tokens' when the figure has expanded to 51 on Luno alone as of March 2026; (5) it does not engage with the legitimate policy rationale for sequencing ringgit tokenisation before addressing foreign-currency stablecoins; (6) it omits Thailand's March 2025 approval of USDT/USDC — the most directly relevant ASEAN comparator — and India's RBI argument that robust domestic payment infrastructure (UPI, and by extension DuitNow) obviates the need for private stablecoins. The analysis would be strengthened by incorporating the distributional consequences of the regulatory architecture, the structural role of BNM's foreign exchange administration, and the experiences of ASEAN peers that have made different policy choices.
